note: Course INTRO videos, audio-driven natural delivery: ElevenLabs voice (Hywjw1pu9qLxpN2CqxU1, Phill McGurk) generates natural-prosody MP3 -> HeyGen Phill avatar lip-syncs to it via /v3/videos audio_url (length follows the speech, no time-boxing). voiceId = ElevenLabs voice; avatarId null -> HEYGEN_AVATAR_ID (Phill). lessonId anchors to the course first lesson only; render writes course.introVideoUrl (+meta) via scripts/apply-intro-video-urls.ts. Scripts grounded per course, no standards-content claims.

briefs.lessonTitle: Water Categories & Classes
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 2: Water Categories & Classes
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner
briefs.script: Welcome to the CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner course. Water damage is one of the most common losses you'll attend in Australia, and the difference between a clean result and a callback usually comes down to the fundamentals. Over the coming modules you'll learn to classify water by category and class, work safely under a hazard-led approach, assess moisture with metric readings, and dry a structure to a defined standard rather than a guess. You'll also build the documentation an insurer relies on, because if it isn't documented, it didn't happen. By the end you'll be ready to run a water-damage job that is safe, effective and defensible. Let's get started.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded in the course's own modules (categories/classes M2, hazard-led safety M3, moisture assessment M4, drying M5, documentation + AU insurance context M6). 'If it isn't documented, it didn't happen' is the course's own M6 line. No standards-content claims (nexus-copywriter standard). en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: What is Odour and Why it Matters
briefs.moduleTitle: What is Odour and Why it Matters
briefs.title: Welcome — Air Quality and Odour
briefs.script: An unresolved odour is often the complaint that brings a technician back to site, so getting deodorisation right matters. This course, Air Quality and Odour, builds the method for identifying and clearing indoor odours. Over the coming modules you'll learn to recognise what odour is and categorise the odours you meet, trace their common indoor sources and their link to volatile organic compounds, and assess and document what you find. You'll work through ventilation and air movement, choose deodorisation methods and equipment setups suited to the job, and manage occupant safety, communication and expectations along the way. By the end you'll be able to verify final odour clearance and hand back a space with confidence. Time to begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: what odour is (M1), categories (M2), common indoor sources (M3), VOCs (M4), assessment/documentation (M5), ventilation/air movement (M6), deodorisation methods (M7), equipment setups (M8), safety/occupant communication/expectations (M9), final clearance and verification (M10). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Understanding Air Quality and Its Importance in Restoration
briefs.moduleTitle: Understanding Air Quality and Its Importance in Restoration
briefs.title: Welcome — Documenting Air Quality Improvements
briefs.script: Good air quality work is only as strong as the record that proves it. Documenting and Reporting Air Quality Improvements shows you how to capture that proof on an Australian restoration job. Over the coming modules you'll learn why air quality matters in restoration, recognise the common contaminants found on job sites, and use the tools and equipment that monitor them. You'll work through the safety measures and protocols for handling poor air quality, then document and report your findings clearly on site. By the end you'll be able to turn field readings into a defensible record that shows the improvement you achieved. Let's make a start.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: importance in restoration (M1), common contaminants (M2), monitoring tools and equipment (M3), safety measures and protocols (M4), documenting and reporting on site (M5). Australian context from short. No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Understanding Indoor Particulates
briefs.moduleTitle: Understanding Indoor Particulates
briefs.title: Welcome — Dust and Particulates in Indoor Air
briefs.script: Dust is easy to overlook and hard to control well, which is exactly why it deserves a considered approach. Dust and Particulates in Indoor Air covers how to assess, measure and manage the fine particles that settle in a building. Over the coming modules you'll learn what indoor particulates are and how they affect health, how dust moves through a space, and how to measure the levels present. You'll compare filtration technologies, apply cleaning strategies, manage dust through HVAC systems, and use source control to stop it at the start. By the end you'll be able to monitor air quality over time and communicate your findings and recommendations clearly. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: understanding particulates (M1), health impacts (M2), how dust moves (M3), measuring levels (M4), filtration technologies (M5), cleaning strategies (M6), HVAC and dust management (M7), source control (M8), monitoring over time (M9), communicating findings (M10). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Understanding the Basics of HVAC and Airflow
briefs.moduleTitle: Understanding the Basics of HVAC and Airflow
briefs.title: Welcome — HVAC Systems and Indoor Air Quality
briefs.script: The HVAC system moves air through a whole building, so it shapes indoor air quality more than almost anything else on site. HVAC Systems and Indoor Air Quality gives every technician the grounding to read that relationship. Over the coming modules you'll learn the basics of HVAC and airflow, recognise common HVAC contaminants, and understand filtration, air cleaning and ventilation. You'll work through maintenance, moisture control and the inspection, cleaning and sealing of ductwork, along with the part HVAC plays in odour control. By the end you'll be able to communicate your IAQ findings and recommend upgrades that improve the air occupants breathe. Let's get into it.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: HVAC and airflow basics (M1), common contaminants (M2), filtration/air cleaning (M3), ventilation (M4), maintenance (M5), moisture control (M6), ductwork inspection/cleaning/sealing (M7), odour control (M8), communicating IAQ findings (M9), IAQ-friendly upgrades (M10). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Understanding Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)
briefs.moduleTitle: Understanding Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)
briefs.title: Welcome — Air Quality Fundamentals
briefs.script: Every restoration and cleaning job affects the air people breathe, and understanding that air is where good work starts. Introduction to Air Quality Fundamentals sets the groundwork for the rest of your air quality training. Over the coming modules you'll learn what indoor air quality is, recognise the common contaminants found in restoration and cleaning work, and understand the principles of ventilation and air exchange. You'll be introduced to the monitoring and assessment tools that measure air quality, and to the strategies that improve and maintain it. By the end you'll have the foundation to approach any air quality task with method rather than guesswork. Ready when you are.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: understanding IAQ (M1), common contaminants in restoration/cleaning (M2), ventilation and air exchange (M3), monitoring and assessment tools (M4), strategies to improve and maintain (M5). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: The Importance of Final Clearance in Air Quality Remediation
briefs.moduleTitle: The Importance of Final Clearance in Air Quality Remediation
briefs.title: Welcome — Creating a Clean Air Environment
briefs.script: The last step of a job is the one the occupant remembers, and final clearance is where the work is proven complete. Creating a Clean Air Environment focuses on that closing stage, from testing through to handover. Over the coming modules you'll learn why final clearance matters, the role air quality testing plays in it, and how to carry out visual inspections and document them for handover. You'll get to know the final clearance equipment and tools, then work through the practices that make a handover clear and reassuring for the client. By the end you'll be able to return a remediated space to its occupants with confidence. Let's step through it.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: importance of final clearance (M1), role of air quality testing (M2), visual inspections and documentation for handover (M3), final clearance equipment and tools (M4), handover and client communication (M5). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Special Design Features
briefs.moduleTitle: Special Design Features
briefs.title: Welcome — Drying Educational and Institutional Sites
briefs.script: Schools, universities and public institutions carry people and shared systems that a drying plan has to work around. Introduction to Drying Educational and Institutional Sites prepares you for exactly those settings. Over the coming modules you'll learn the special design features these buildings present, how their shared infrastructure shapes your approach, and how building population factors affect a live site. You'll consider the regulatory oversight that applies and the loss limitation tactics that keep a small event from becoming a large one. By the end you'll be able to plan and manage drying in an educational or institutional site while protecting the infrastructure everyone depends on. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: special design features (M1), shared infrastructure (M2), building population factors (M3), regulatory oversight (M4), loss limitation tactics (M5). Protecting shared infrastructure and regulatory requirements from short. Mismatched healthcare sample ignored. No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Special Design Considerations
briefs.moduleTitle: Special Design Considerations
briefs.title: Welcome — Drying Health Care Facilities
briefs.script: Few environments demand more care during drying than a working health care facility, where patients and critical systems can't simply pause. Introduction to Drying Health Care Facilities builds the awareness that setting requires. Over the coming modules you'll learn the special design considerations of these buildings, the unique building systems you'll encounter, and the infection control risks that must guide every decision. You'll look at how to keep critical operations running and at the remediation standards that apply to this work. By the end you'll understand how to approach drying a water-damaged health care facility with the caution and method it calls for. Let's get underway.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: special design considerations (M1), unique building systems (M2), infection control risks (M3), maintaining critical operations (M4), remediation standards named as a topic only, no standard content asserted (M5). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Business Operations Impacts
briefs.moduleTitle: Business Operations Impacts
briefs.title: Welcome — Drying Hospitality and Lodging Sites
briefs.script: In hospitality, every closed room is lost revenue, so drying work has to move fast without cutting corners. Introduction to Drying Hospitality and Lodging Sites shows you how to hold that balance. Over the coming modules you'll learn the business operations impacts of a water event, how to handle room and content considerations, and how to stage structural repairs around a working venue. You'll work through the reconstructive constraints these sites impose and the remediation cleanup that returns rooms to service. By the end you'll be able to manage a hospitality restoration that limits revenue loss and protects the contents guests expect to find intact. Let's make a start.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: business operations impacts (M1), room and content considerations (M2), structural repair staging (M3), reconstructive constraints (M4), remediation cleanup (M5). Limiting revenue loss and protecting room contents from short. No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Production Considerations
briefs.moduleTitle: Production Considerations
briefs.title: Welcome — Drying Industrial and Manufacturing Sites
briefs.script: Industrial and manufacturing sites combine live production, complex materials and real hazards, which makes drying them a discipline of its own. Introduction to Drying Industrial and Manufacturing Sites prepares you for that complexity. Over the coming modules you'll learn the production considerations that shape your plan, how hazard communication keeps a workforce safe, and how to reconstitute operations after a loss. You'll work through drying the complex materials these sites contain and the steps toward achieving regulatory compliance. By the end you'll be able to approach an industrial drying project methodically, keeping people safe and production moving. Let's get to work.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to modules: production considerations (M1), hazard communication (M2), reconstituting operations (M3), drying complex materials (M4), achieving regulatory compliance (M5). Production impacts and regulatory compliance from short. No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Production Considerations
briefs.moduleTitle: Production Considerations
briefs.title: Welcome — Drying Transportation and Vehicles
briefs.script: A flood-affected vehicle hides its damage in wiring looms, cavities and materials you can't see, so restoring one safely takes a careful eye. Introduction to Drying Transportation and Vehicles walks you through that work. Over the coming modules you'll learn to manage the electrical damage, corrosion and contaminants a submerged vehicle carries, communicate the hazards involved, and dry the complex materials packed into a vehicle body. You'll work toward regulatory compliance and, above all, monitor and validate that a vehicle is safe to function again. By the end you'll be able to assess whether a flood-affected vehicle can be restored and see the job through. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Claims trace to short (electrical damage, corrosion, contaminants, monitoring and validating safe function) plus modules: hazard communication (M2), drying complex materials (M4), achieving regulatory compliance (M5). No standards-content claims; en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: The Impact of Mould on Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)
briefs.moduleTitle: The Impact of Mould on Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)
briefs.title: Welcome — IAQ and Mould: Airborne Spread and Containment
briefs.script: Welcome to the CARSI course on indoor air quality and mould. When mould takes hold indoors, the risk to the people in a building often travels through the air long before it is seen, which makes containment as important as removal on any Australian job. Over the coming modules you'll learn to recognise how mould affects indoor air quality, understand the ways spores spread through the air, set up containment that stops that spread, apply remediation techniques for mould and airborne spores, and monitor the air once the work is done. By the end you'll be able to approach a mould-affected space with a clear plan to contain, remediate and verify. Let's make a start.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: mould affects IAQ (M1), spread through air (M2), containment (M3), remediation of mould and airborne spores (M4), monitoring/maintaining IAQ after remediation (M5).

briefs.lessonTitle: The Impact of Water Damage on Indoor Air Quality
briefs.moduleTitle: The Impact of Water Damage on Indoor Air Quality
briefs.title: Welcome — Improving Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage
briefs.script: Water damage doesn't end when the water is gone — the air in a building can carry the consequences for far longer, and restoring it is part of a complete result. This CARSI course introduces how water damage affects indoor air quality and what a technician can do about it. Over the coming modules you'll learn to understand the impact of water damage on indoor air quality, identify the contaminants that commonly appear afterwards, use tools and techniques to assess the air, apply remediation techniques that improve it, and document and report the improvements you've made. By the end you'll be ready to return a water-damaged space to healthy air and prove the result on paper. Let's get started.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: impact of water damage on IAQ (M1), identifying contaminants (M2), tools/techniques for assessing IAQ (M3), remediation techniques (M4), documenting and reporting improvements (M5).

briefs.lessonTitle: Thermal Energy Concepts
briefs.moduleTitle: Thermal Energy Concepts
briefs.title: Welcome — Infrared Thermography for Drying
briefs.script: An infrared camera can show a technician what the eye alone cannot — the cool signature of moisture hidden inside a structure. This CARSI course introduces infrared thermography and how it guides a drying job. Over the coming modules you'll learn the thermal energy concepts behind the images, how infrared camera technologies work, how to apply them to moisture detection, how to use thermography safely, and how to analyse the results and put them to work on site. By the end you'll be able to read a thermal image with a clear understanding of what it is telling you, and let it direct where drying effort is needed. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: cool moisture signature (short — cameras detect moisture, read thermal images to guide drying); thermal energy concepts (M1), IR camera technologies (M2), moisture detection applications (M3), safely applying thermography (M4), result analysis and applications (M5).

briefs.lessonTitle: Evaluating Restorability
briefs.moduleTitle: Evaluating Restorability
briefs.title: Welcome — Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents
briefs.script: Not every water-damaged item can be saved, and knowing the difference is where good contents work begins. The decisions made early can be the difference between an item recovered and one written off. This CARSI course introduces the recovery of submerged items and contents after a water loss. Over the coming modules you'll learn to evaluate whether an item is restorable, manage the logistics of recovery, apply initial preservation to buy time, select appropriate cleaning methods, and validate that a restored item works as it should. By the end you'll be able to move through a contents job with sound judgement about what to recover and how to confirm it has been returned to use. Then let's get into it.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: evaluating restorability (M1), recovery logistics (M2), initial preservation (M3), cleaning methods (M4), functionality validation (M5); 'restorable vs written off' traces to short + M1.

briefs.lessonTitle: Material Integrity Risks
briefs.moduleTitle: Material Integrity Risks
briefs.title: Welcome — Restoration of Antiques and Fine Furnishings
briefs.script: Antique furniture rewards care and punishes haste, and moisture damage raises the stakes on both. A single wrong method can cost a piece its value, which is why judgement matters as much as technique. This CARSI course introduces the restoration of moisture-affected antiques and fine furnishings. Over the coming modules you'll learn to recognise the material integrity risks these pieces face, apply the right handling precautions, select cleaning techniques suited to delicate surfaces, work through sound restorative methodologies, and understand the role of authentication, appraisals and provenance. By the end you'll be able to approach a valuable piece with the caution it deserves and document its history along the way. Ready when you are.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: material integrity risks (M1), handling precautions (M2), cleaning technique selection (M3), restorative methodologies (M4), authentication and appraisals (M5); provenance/documenting from short + M5.

briefs.lessonTitle: Ultraviolet Light Spectrum
briefs.moduleTitle: Ultraviolet Light Spectrum
briefs.title: Welcome — Ultraviolet Light and Fluorescence
briefs.script: Under the right light, a water loss can reveal detail that stays hidden in ordinary daylight. Ultraviolet inspection gives a technician another way to find moisture and signs of microbial growth. This CARSI course introduces ultraviolet light and fluorescence as inspection tools in water damage work. Over the coming modules you'll learn about the ultraviolet light spectrum, the principles of fluorescence, how to apply the equipment, how UV supports water loss inspections, and how it works alongside complementary technologies. By the end you'll be able to use ultraviolet equipment safely and read what it shows you as part of a thorough inspection. Let's get started.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: UV detects moisture and microbial growth, use equipment safely (short); UV light spectrum (M1), fluorescence principles (M2), equipment application (M3), water loss inspection uses (M4), complementary technologies (M5).

briefs.lessonTitle: Forensic Investigations
briefs.moduleTitle: Forensic Investigations
briefs.title: Welcome — Water Damage Litigation Support
briefs.script: When a water damage claim ends up in dispute, the quality of the evidence is what holds up. A restoration technician is often the person best placed to record what actually happened on site. This CARSI course introduces the forensic side of water damage work and how it supports litigation. Over the coming modules you'll learn how forensic investigations trace a loss, how to gather data that stands scrutiny, how to analyse the scope of damage, how to create reports that carry weight, and what is involved in legal proceedings and expert testimony. By the end you'll understand how careful investigation and clear documentation become evidence a matter can rely on. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: forensic investigations (M1), data gathering (M2), scope analysis (M3), report creation (M4), legal proceedings (M5); tracing sources/expert testimony from short.

briefs.lessonTitle: The Moisture-Mould Connection
briefs.moduleTitle: The Moisture-Mould Connection
briefs.title: Welcome — Moisture, Mould and Indoor Air Quality
briefs.script: Moisture, mould and the air people breathe are closely linked, and understanding that link is the foundation of healthy indoor work. This CARSI course draws the connections together. Over the coming modules you'll learn how moisture drives mould growth, how to identify common moisture sources, how mould affects indoor air quality, which building materials are most susceptible, and the role humidity and HVAC systems play. You'll go on to cover the health effects of mould exposure, inspection protocols and air sampling, drying strategies that prevent regrowth, remediation approaches that recover air quality, and clear communication and documentation. By the end you'll see the whole chain from a damp source to healthy air restored. Let's make a start.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: moisture-mould connection (M1), moisture sources (M2), mould affects IAQ (M3), material susceptibility (M4), humidity/HVAC (M5), health effects (M6), inspection & air sampling (M7), drying to prevent regrowth (M8), remediation & air recovery (M9), communication & documentation (M10).

briefs.lessonTitle: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers
briefs.moduleTitle: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers
briefs.title: Welcome — Air Scrubbers and AFDs
briefs.script: That large machine humming in the corner of a job site is doing real work, and using it well is a skill worth learning. This CARSI course introduces air filtration devices and air scrubbers and how they improve job-site air quality. Over the coming modules you'll learn what AFDs and air scrubbers are, their components and mechanics, the types of filters and what each does, the difference between recirculation and negative pressure, correct setup and placement, how to calculate air changes per hour, filter maintenance, safety and compliance, and how to monitor effectiveness and document your work for the client. By the end you'll be able to set up, run and verify an air scrubber with confidence. Let's get into it.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded: intro to AFDs/air scrubbers (M1), components and mechanics (M2), filter types (M3), recirculation vs negative pressure (M4), setup and placement (M5), air changes per hour (M6), maintenance (M7), safety and compliance (M8), monitoring effectiveness (M9), client communication and documentation (M10).

briefs.lessonTitle: Water Damage Fundamentals
briefs.moduleTitle: Water Damage Fundamentals
briefs.title: Welcome — Water Damage Restoration: Essentials
briefs.script: Every water damage job runs on a handful of fundamentals, and this essentials course sets them out from the ground up. Water is one of the most common losses attended in Australia, and a steady, methodical approach is what separates a clean finish from a return visit. Over the coming modules you'll learn to work safely with standards and site control, understand water categories and the material science behind them, carry out initial inspection and moisture mapping, apply water extraction principles, manage evaporation and dehumidification, select and place equipment, and cover monitoring, psychrometry basics, completion and handover. By the end you'll have the grounding to take a water-damage job from first inspection through to a documented sign-off. Time to begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Grounded in this course's own modules (distinct from pilot): safety/standards/site control (M1), water categories & material science (M2), initial inspection & moisture mapping (M3), extraction principles (M4), evaporation & dehumidification (M5), equipment selection & placement (M6), monitoring & psychrometry basics (M7), completion/handover/reporting (M8); documented sign-off from sample.

briefs.lessonTitle: Official message and reporting pathway
briefs.moduleTitle: Official message and reporting pathway
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI H5 Bird Flu Awareness
briefs.script: This is the CARSI H5 Bird Flu Awareness course for restoration, IAQ and facility professionals. When avian influenza is in the news, calm and accurate handling on site matters as much as any technical skill. This is awareness training: your role is to report correctly, protect workers, document your field work and communicate clearly, not to take on work beyond your scope. Over the coming modules you'll learn to follow the official reporting pathway, understand your professional role and its boundaries, work safely with PPE awareness and clean and dirty zones, apply cleaning before disinfection, document through the RestoreAssist.app pathway, and communicate calmly with clients, media and the public. By the end you'll know how to respond within scope, safely and professionally.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Awareness/report-and-document scope from short + M2 (role, scope, boundaries); reporting pathway M1; PPE/clean-dirty zones M3; cleaning-before-disinfection M4; RestoreAssist.app documentation M5; client/media/social comms M6. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner
briefs.script: Welcome to the CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner course. Mould is a familiar problem on Australian restoration jobs, and a professional result depends on working to a clear sequence rather than simply cleaning what you can see. Over the coming modules you'll learn to understand mould biology and its health considerations, assess a site as Condition 1, 2 or 3, set up containment and engineering controls, follow the correct remediation sequence, match PPE to the condition you're working in, and verify and document the outcome. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be ready to remediate mould methodically and prove the area is returned to a clean condition. Let's begin.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Biology/health M2; Condition 1/2/3 assessment M3; containment/engineering controls M4; remediation sequence M5; PPE by condition M6; verification + documentation M7 (verified-to-Condition-1 from sample). CEC-accredited + designation from short. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner
briefs.script: The CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner course is about drying a building to a defined standard, not to a guess. On Australian jobs the difference between a dry structure and a returning problem comes down to the science and the readings behind your decisions. Over the coming modules you'll learn to set a clear drying goal, use psychrometry to make drying decisions, place air movers for effective airflow, select and size dehumidification, design a drying system around 230-volt power and electrical safety, and monitor with consistent readings so you know when to adjust. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be ready to run a drying job you can prove worked.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Setting the drying goal M2; psychrometry for drying decisions M3; airflow/air movers M4; dehumidification M5; 230V/10A drying-system design + electrical safety M6 (also short); monitoring/consistent readings M7 (from sample). CEC + designation from short. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner
briefs.script: This is the CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner course, covering Category 3 sewage and black-water remediation. Contaminated-water losses are among the higher-risk jobs an Australian technician attends, and they demand a hazard-led approach from the first minute on site. Over the coming modules you'll learn what makes Category 3 water different, assess the health hazards and risks, select PPE and protect yourself, contain, remove, clean and sanitise the affected area, dispose of waste safely within the Australian regulatory context, and dry, verify and document a clean structure. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be ready to handle a contaminated-water job safely, methodically and defensibly.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: What Cat 3 is/why different M2; health hazards + risk assessment M3; PPE/personal safety M4; containment/removal/cleaning/sanitising M5; safe disposal + AU regulatory context M6; drying/verification/documentation M7 (dry a clean structure from sample). CEC + designation from short. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Asbestos Awareness for Restoration
briefs.script: This is the CARSI Asbestos Awareness course for restoration technicians. Asbestos can still be present in many older Australian buildings, and knowing how to recognise and respond to it is a core part of working safely. This is awareness training, so it prepares you to recognise and respond, not to disturb, handle or remove asbestos, which is licensed work. Over the coming modules you'll learn what asbestos is, understand the health risk and the Australian context, know your legal duties at an awareness level, and recognise likely asbestos-containing materials so you can stop, avoid disturbing them, isolate the area and escalate correctly. By the end you'll know how to respond to suspected asbestos and where to get help.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Awareness-only + stop/do-not-disturb/isolate/escalate-to-licensed from short + M5; what asbestos is M2; health risk + Australian ban M3; legal duties (awareness level) M4; where to get help M6. Explicitly does not imply licensing. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Psychrometry & Building Science
briefs.script: Welcome to CARSI Psychrometry and Building Science for Drying. Every reliable drying decision rests on understanding the air, and this course builds that foundation for work in Australian conditions. Over the coming modules you'll learn to work with temperature, relative humidity, dew point and specific humidity in grams per kilogram, read a hygrometer and a psychrometric chart, understand vapour pressure and how water moves from materials into the air, apply the dry standard and equilibrium moisture content, and prove drying is working through consistent daily readings. You'll also see how Bureau of Meteorology climatology and climate zones shape realistic drying expectations. By the end you'll read the air with confidence and make drying decisions you can defend.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Temp/RH/dew point/specific humidity g/kg M2; hygrometer + psychrometric chart M3; vapour pressure + water movement M4; dry standard + EMC M5; daily readings M6; BoM climatology + NCC climate zones M7 (realistic expectations from sample). No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner
briefs.script: Welcome to the CARSI Fire and Smoke Restoration Practitioner course. Fire and smoke losses are among the more complex jobs an Australian restorer attends, where the right cleaning method depends on reading the damage correctly. Over the coming modules you'll learn to understand how fire, smoke and soot behave, identify the residue types and select the matching cleaning method, follow the fire and smoke restoration process, assess HVAC impact and affected contents, recognise the urgency of corrosion and approach structural deodorisation, and work safely with PPE around Australian-specific hazards. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Fire and Smoke Restoration Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be ready to plan and carry out a fire and smoke job with method and care.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Fire/smoke/soot behaviour M2; residue types + cleaning-method selection M3; restoration process M4; HVAC impact + contents assessment M5; corrosion urgency + structural deodorisation M6; safety/PPE/AU-specific hazards M7. CEC + designation from short. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner
briefs.script: This is the CARSI Trauma and Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner course. This is sensitive work carried out with care, discretion and a clear duty of care to the people affected, and it calls for method and composure in equal measure. Over the coming modules you'll learn to understand the nature of the work and the discretion it demands, recognise bloodborne pathogens and biohazard risk, complete a risk assessment and safe work method statement, select PPE and decontaminate safely, contain, remove, clean and sanitise the affected area, and dispose of waste lawfully, while looking after your own psychological safety. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Trauma and Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be prepared to approach this work safely, lawfully and with respect.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Nature of work + duty of care + discretion M2; bloodborne pathogens/biohazard risk M3; risk assessment + SWMS M4; PPE + safe decontamination M5; containment/removal/cleaning/sanitising M6; lawful disposal + regulatory context + worker psychological safety M7. CEC + designation from short. Measured tone. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.moduleTitle: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works
briefs.title: Welcome — CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner
briefs.script: Welcome to the CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner course. A sound assessment is the foundation of good restoration decisions, and it depends on measuring the right things and knowing how to interpret them. Over the coming modules you'll learn what indoor environmental quality is and the assessor's role, view the building as a system, decide what to measure and with which instruments, apply a consistent inspection method and map your findings, interpret readings and recognise mould, and, importantly, work within your competency limits to produce a defensible report. This CEC-accredited training leads to the CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner designation. By the end you'll be ready to assess an indoor environment methodically and report it in a way that stands up to scrutiny.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: IEQ + assessor's role M2 (edge-of-competency from sample); building as a system M3; what to measure/instruments M4; inspection method + mapping M5; interpreting readings + recognising mould M6; competency limits + defensible report M7. CEC + designation from short. No standards-content claims. en-AU.

briefs.lessonTitle: Before you begin — important notice (read first)
briefs.moduleTitle: Before you begin — important notice (read first)
briefs.title: Welcome — WHS Fundamentals for Restoration & Cleaning
briefs.script: This is the CARSI Work Health and Safety Fundamentals course for restoration and cleaning professionals. Safe work is the ground every restoration job stands on, and this course builds a practical foundation for Australian sites. It is an educational overview rather than legal advice. Over the coming modules you'll learn about the WHS framework that applies to you in Australia, work through the risk management process to find and control hazards, recognise hazardous materials such as asbestos and silica on strip-out sites, use PPE and respiratory protection, handle site-specific hazards including wet-site electrical, confined spaces, biohazard and mould, manage decontamination, zones and waste, and respond to emergencies, first aid and incident reporting. By the end you'll approach restoration and cleaning work with safety front of mind.
briefs.visualDirection: Presenter to camera, CARSI slate background; lower-third with course title on the opening line.
briefs.authorNote: Educational-not-legal-advice from sample + M1; WHS framework that applies in Australia M2 (topical, no content claim); risk management process M3; hazardous materials asbestos/silica M4 (from short); PPE/respiratory M5; site-specific wet-site electrical/confined spaces/biohazard/mould M6 (from short); decontamination/zones/waste M7; emergencies/first aid/incident reporting M8. No standards-content claims. en-AU.
